deductions.reason: Across all three conversations, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—i.e., it never articulates a rationale such as 'I'm asking you to work this out because active reasoning builds retention' or similar. The tutoring constraints are enforced but the pedagogical rationale is never stated.

deductions.evidence_turns: Transcript 1, Turns 1-10; Transcript 2, Turn 10; Transcript 3, Turn 10
deductions.reason: In all three transcripts, when students make meaningful progress (e.g., T1 student correctly identifies governance rationale for city proper; T2 student correctly articulates the proxy-labeling problem; T3 student produces a solid three-category framework), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved reasoning worked.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts the tutor performs strongly on the core Socratic and scaffolding dimensions: it never gives submission-ready answers, consistently redirects students to their own reasoning, builds on student progress, and maintains appropriate tone and concise responses. The primary gap is in meta-learning: the tutor never explains why it is guiding rather than answering (no stated pedagogical rationale), and it consistently misses opportunities to prompt reflection after students make meaningful improvements.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 and 1.2 are clean: the tutor uses questions throughout, never provides final answers or rewrites student text, builds on each student's prior reasoning, and diagnoses confusion before responding. For 1.3, two deductions apply across all three transcripts. First (1.3.A.a): the tutor enforces a guided-discovery approach in every conversation but never once explains to the student why it is doing so—no statement about active learning, retention, or skill-building appears anywhere. Second (1.3.C.a): at multiple points where students visibly improve (T1 Turn 10 governance reasoning, T2 Turn 9 proxy methodology, T3 Turn 9 three-category framework), the tutor simply advances to the next task without asking the student to reflect on what changed or why the new reasoning was stronger. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both full marks: responses are bite-sized, tone is consistently supportive and non-evaluative, there is no off-topic drift, and no assignment misunderstanding goes uncorrected.
